current_fact.fact_text: On this day in 2021, the funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, took place at St George’s Chapel, Windsor Castle, under strict pandemic restrictions. The image of Queen Elizabeth II sitting alone in a pew became an emblem of both personal loss and a global moment of isolation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

current_fact.match_reason: A restrained, symbolic royal funeral during a time of global stillness fits a reflective episode about vulnerability, transition, and looking back at difficult chapters with tenderness.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 1964, French prisoner of war General Henri Giraud’s escape wasn’t the only high‑risk venture; that same era saw countless others attempt breakouts from fortress‑like prisons, but Giraud’s success at Königstein earned him a mythic aura. His story became a reminder that even engineered "no‑escape" systems are vulnerable to patience and ingenuity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: Revisiting Giraud's story as a broader symbol of escaping rigid systems pairs with a "Slow Planet" episode that invites stepping outside our own built constraints into a slower, freer rhythm.

queue.fact_text: Also on this date in 1941, the swift end of the invasion of Yugoslavia echoed across Europe as a cautionary tale about underestimating fast, mechanized warfare. The kingdom’s collapse in less than two weeks showed how quickly borders, alliances, and whole maps can be redrawn.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A war that shattered old patterns and rewrote borders reflects an episode introducing a format with "no rules" and shifting textures, both marking turning points into new, less predictable terrain.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 2014, when Kepler‑186f was announced, astronomers quietly noted that it's part of a system where the host star is much cooler and redder than our Sun, meaning any plants there might evolve black or dark pigments to capture light. It was one of the first times scientists seriously described what alien photosynthesis could look like.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Speculating about alien ecosystems beneath a red sun suits a slow‑building, otherworldly evening mix that starts mysterious and blooms into vibrant, global life.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1998, the STS‑90 "Neurolab" mission carried not just human experiments but also rats, fish, crickets, and snails into orbit to see how gravity—or the lack of it—shapes living brains. Some animals showed that their nervous systems rewired themselves within days, offering a glimpse of how adaptable life can be far from Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A mission studying animal brains in weightlessness aligns with an episode about intimate encounters with intelligent marine animals, both exploring how nonhuman nervous systems adapt in extreme environments.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 2014, NASA’s Kepler space telescope confirmed Kepler‑186f, the first known Earth‑size planet orbiting within the habitable zone of another star. This rocky world orbits a red dwarf 500 light years away and receives about a third of the light Earth gets, making its skies glow in a dim red twilight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A groundbreaking exoplanet discovery pairs naturally with an episode about rethinking space and seeing emptiness as potential, underpinned by energetic, forward-looking tracks.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1970, the crew of Apollo 13 safely returned to Earth after an explosion crippled their spacecraft en route to the Moon. For four tense days, three astronauts survived in a freezing lunar module jury‑rigged as a lifeboat, while engineers on the ground improvised life‑saving fixes in real time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: Apollo 13 is a story of near-disaster turned hopeful return, resonating with an episode themed around weathering storms and emerging into a more hopeful chapter.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1998, Space Shuttle Columbia launched STS‑90, the final Spacelab mission, turning the orbiter into a fully fledged orbiting neuroscience laboratory. For 16 days, astronauts and experimental animals floated in weightlessness while scientists studied how the brain and nervous system adapt to space.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A quietly experimental space-biology mission fits the moody, boundary-blurring atmosphere of a trip-hop deep dive, both exploring altered states and new environments.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1492, the Capitulations of Santa Fe were signed between the Spanish Crown and Christopher Columbus, granting him titles, riches, and the right to claim lands in exchange for reaching Asia by sailing west. The agreement helped launch a voyage that accidentally revealed the Americas to Europe and reshaped global history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A royal contract that precedes a world‑altering voyage pairs with an episode about travel and perspective shifts, soundtracked by globally tinged, wandering grooves.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1783, at the remote Arkansas Post, a small Spanish garrison under Captain Jacobo du Breuil repelled British irregulars in Colbert’s Raid, one of the last skirmishes of the American Revolutionary War. The encounter highlighted how far‑flung colonial outposts, not just major cities, helped determine the war’s outcome.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A small, overlooked frontier battle shaping a larger war matches a reflective episode about quiet places and subtle undercurrents, carried by intricate jazz rather than bombast.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1524, Florentine explorer Giovanni da Verrazzano sailed into what is now New York Harbor, becoming the first recorded European to see the site of the future city. He described it as a "very agreeable" bay rimmed with forests and inhabited by curious Lenape people who approached his ship in canoes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

published.match_reason: Verrazzano’s first impressions of a harbor that would become a mega‑city pair with a city‑walk episode, shifting from first contact with a landscape to intimate musical postcards from another urban giant.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1797, the citizens of Verona launched the Veronese Easter uprising, an eight‑day rebellion against French occupation during the French Revolutionary Wars. Armed with whatever they could find, townspeople turned a festive religious period into a desperate fight for local autonomy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An Italian city rising up against occupation matches an episode rooted in Italian identity and contrasting energies, with fiery songwriters echoing the spirit of rebellion.

published.fact_text: On this date in 1863, Union cavalry under Colonel Benjamin Grierson began Grierson’s Raid, a daring 600‑mile ride through Confederate Mississippi. Moving fast and unexpectedly, his troopers tore up railroads and sowed confusion, helping clear the way for the Union capture of Vicksburg.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A relentless cavalry raid defined by motion and surprise fits an "on the move" episode packed with rolling, forward‑driving grooves.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1895, the Treaty of Shimonoseki was signed, ending the First Sino‑Japanese War and forcing the Qing Empire to cede Taiwan, the Penghu Islands, and influence over Korea to Japan. The treaty dramatically shifted power in East Asia and marked Japan’s emergence as a modern imperial force.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A treaty that forced empires to rethink their place in Asia pairs with an episode about unlearning and shifting perspectives, even featuring Japanese music that hints at the region’s cultural complexity.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1912, Russian troops fired on striking goldfield workers along the Lena River in Siberia, killing at least 150 people in what became known as the Lena massacre. The brutal crackdown shocked the empire and helped reignite revolutionary anger that would culminate a few years later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A massacre of striking workers that fuels revolution fits an episode centered on politically charged music and structural anger against injustice.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1941, the invasion of Yugoslavia ended when the royal Yugoslav government signed an armistice with Germany and Italy after just 11 days of fighting. The country was dismembered and occupied, setting the stage for a brutal resistance war and one of Europe’s most complex World War II frontlines.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: The dismemberment and occupation of a country that sparks resistance echoes in an episode about Tuareg music born from exile, occupation, and the will to survive.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1942, French General Henri Giraud mounted a daring escape from the supposedly inescapable Königstein Fortress, where he was held as a German prisoner of war. Using a makeshift rope made from bedsheets, he descended a steep cliff and evaded capture long enough to return to Allied territory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A cliffside prison break and sprint to freedom fits the adrenaline and high-voltage guitar energy of an "Electric Shock" rock episode.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1944, fighters of the Greek People's Liberation Army (ELAS) attacked the smaller National and Social Liberation resistance group, which surrendered after heavy fighting; its leader Dimitrios Psarros was then murdered. The clash revealed how even anti‑occupation movements can fracture along ideological lines.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A tragic civil clash within occupied Greece links with an episode sailing the Mediterranean, featuring Greek music that hints at the region’s layered, sometimes conflicting histories.

published.fact_text: On this date in 1941, the rule of the Bavand dynasty in Iran’s Mazandaran region ended with the assassination of Hasan II, its last ruler. The dynasty had survived in the shadow of great empires for nearly 700 years, outliving the early caliphs, Mongols, and other conquerors before finally disappearing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: The quiet end of a long‑lived regional dynasty mirrors an episode centered on a specific region and its deep-rooted identity, with folk textures reflecting local continuity and loss.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1964, American pilot Jerrie Mock completed the first solo round‑the‑world flight by a woman, landing her Cessna "Spirit of Columbus" back in Ohio after 29½ days. Nicknamed the "1964 housewife pilot" by the press, she calmly navigated storms, mechanical issues, and unfamiliar cultures with almost no fanfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A pioneering solo circumnavigation pairs well with a seafaring, journey-focused episode; both celebrate long routes, open horizons, and quiet resilience in motion.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1971, amid a brutal war of independence, the Provisional Government of Bangladesh was formally established. Operating in exile, it provided a political backbone to the Bengali freedom movement that would soon create a new nation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: The birth of a provisional government and a national identity aligns with an episode about Residente, an artist whose work is steeped in political critique, resistance, and the search for self-determination.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1975, the Khmer Rouge entered Phnom Penh, ending the Cambodian Civil War and beginning the Cambodian Genocide. Within days, the city’s entire population was forced into the countryside as the regime attempted a radical social experiment that would kill roughly a quarter of the country’s people.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Such a devastating "social experiment" gone wrong contrasts sharply with an episode about healthy learning and growth, prompting reflection on how powerfully—and differently—ideas can reshape human lives.

published.fact_text: On this date in 1978, Afghan leftist intellectual Mir Akbar Khyber was assassinated in Kabul, an event that sparked mass protests and directly triggered the Saur Revolution days later. His death helped topple the government and plunge Afghanistan into decades of coups, invasions, and conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A single assassination catalyzing a national upheaval echoes an episode about how one missing object (a phone) can dramatically change perception and behavior, both centering on triggering events that shift entire systems.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1992, the Greek tanker Katina P was deliberately run aground off Maputo, Mozambique, spilling around 60,000 tons of crude oil. The accident blackened beaches, killed marine life, and underscored how distant shipping decisions can devastate fragile coastal ecosystems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An oil spill that scars the sea contrasts poignantly with an episode about immersion in clear water and fluidity, highlighting both the beauty and vulnerability of marine environments.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2021, the funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, took place at St George’s Chapel, Windsor Castle, under strict pandemic restrictions. The image of Queen Elizabeth II sitting alone in a pew became an emblem of both personal loss and a global moment of isolation.
